title: Smart Gardens: Automated Plant Watering!
subtitle: code, sense, grow!
overview: Students will design, build, and program an automated plant watering system using a soil moisture sensor and a water pump, exploring how technology solves real-world ecological challenges.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — component photos, activity shots, student experiments, whiteboard diagrams.

keyTopics: Soil Moisture Sensor — Sponge Analogy | Wet soil (easy flow) vs Dry soil (blocked flow)
Relay Module — The Safe Bridge | Electronic switch separating low power (Arduino) from high power (Pump)
Submersible Pump — The Mechanical Straw | Moves water uphill against gravity
Calibration — Finding the Sweet Spot | Reading values for 'thirsty' vs 'happy' plants
Automation Loop — Check Soil → Is it dry? → Yes? Water! → No? Wait.
Water Conservation — Using technology to prevent wastage | Hindi: Jal Sanrakshan

activity.title: Smart Plant Watering System Build
parts: Two cups (one with dry soil, one with wet soil)
steps: Connect the Soil Moisture Sensor to Arduino Analog Pin A0, VCC, and GND.
Test sensor values in dry soil vs wet soil using the Serial Monitor.
Connect the Relay Module input pin to Arduino Digital Pin 8.
Wire the mini water pump to the external power source through the Relay's Normally Open (NO) contacts.
Upload the control code: If sensor reading is high (dry), turn Relay ON; else, turn Relay OFF.
Place the pump in water, tube in the dry soil, and watch it water itself until happy!
Challenge: Add a buzzer alarm that sounds if the water reservoir is completely empty.

quiz.q: What does the Soil Moisture Sensor actually measure to guess how much water is in the dirt?
options: The temperature of the mud
Electrical resistance / conductivity of the soil
The weight of the flower pot
The color of the soil
quiz.explanation: Water conducts electricity! Wet soil allows current to flow easily (low resistance), while dry soil blocks it (high resistance).

quiz.q: Why do we use a RELAY module instead of connecting the water pump directly to the Arduino pins?
options: The pump needs too much current/power, which can damage or overload the Arduino pins
The relay makes the water cleaner
The pump cannot understand digital code without a relay
To change the color of the pump
quiz.explanation: Pumps have motors that draw a lot of electrical current. Arduino pins can only supply a tiny amount of power. The relay acts as an electronic switch to safely pass higher power from an external battery/supply.

quiz.q: In our code loop, what is the correct logic for an automated watering machine?
options: If soil is WET → Turn pump ON
If soil is DRY → Turn pump ON
Keep pump ON all the time forever
Turn pump ON only when the sun goes down
quiz.explanation: We only want to water the plant when it is thirsty! So, If soil is DRY → Turn pump ON. Once it becomes wet, the pump should turn off automatically.

quiz.q: What is the process of testing your sensor in dry soil and wet soil to find the target code values called?
options: Acceleration
Evaporation
Calibration
Combustion
quiz.explanation: Calibration means tuning or adjusting an instrument so it reads correctly. Finding your specific sensor's 'dry' and 'wet' numbers on the monitor is calibrating it!

quiz.q: What is the primary ecological benefit of an automated watering system?
options: It makes plants grow blue leaves
It eliminates the need for sunlight
It conserves water by giving plants only what they need, reducing waste
It charges the Arduino using soil power
quiz.explanation: Smart irrigation saves water (Jal Sanrakshan)! It stops watering the moment the soil is damp enough, preventing overwatering and runoff.
